title: Ethambutol optic neuropathy (anti-TB)
vignette: A 45-year-old man comes to the office due to 3 months of night sweats, productive cough, decreased appetite, and a 5-kg (10-lb) weight loss. He travels frequently to Vietnam. Vital signs are normal. Examination reveals crackles in the right upper lobe of the lung. Chest x-ray reveals a right upper lobe infiltrate. Acid-fast bacilli are present on sputum smear. The patient is started on combination therapy with isoniazid, ethambutol, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and pyridoxine. One month later, he returns to the office reporting blurring of vision. Examination shows bilateral central scotomas. Which of the following is most likely responsible for this patient's visual symptoms?

clues.text: 3 months of night sweats, productive cough, decreased appetite
clues.note: Chronic cough with night sweats, anorexia, and weight loss is the constitutional triad of pulmonary tuberculosis (a subacute infection), not an acute bacterial pneumonia.
Frequent travel to a TB-endemic country is a major epidemiologic risk factor. Exposure in a high-prevalence region raises the pretest probability of tuberculosis sharply.

clues.text: crackles in the right upper lobe of the lung
clues.note: Apical/upper-lobe disease is classic for reactivation TB, which favors the well-oxygenated lung apices. The location itself is a clue to the organism.
An upper-lobe infiltrate on chest x-ray is the textbook radiographic pattern of reactivation pulmonary TB, matching the apical crackles on exam.

clues.text: Acid-fast bacilli are present on sputum smear
clues.note: Acid-fast bacilli on sputum smear confirm mycobacterial infection (M. tuberculosis). This locks in the diagnosis and the need for multidrug RIPE therapy.

clues.text: isoniazid, ethambutol, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and pyridoxine
clues.note: This is the standard 4-drug RIPE regimen (rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) plus pyridoxine (B6) to prevent isoniazid neuropathy. The question turns on which of these drugs is toxic to the eye.
New visual blurring AFTER starting therapy points to a drug adverse effect, not the TB itself. This reframes the question as a pharmacology/toxicity item.
Bilateral central scotomas (with decreased acuity / color vision loss) are the hallmark of optic neuropathy. Among the TB drugs, ethambutol is the one that classically injures the optic nerve.

whatMatters: The real ask is NOT 'what is the disease' (clearly reactivation TB) but 'which of his RIPE drugs caused new bilateral central scotomas.' Strip the TB story to background; the testable point is the drug→toxicity match. Decreased vision + central scotoma = optic neuropathy = ethambutol. Answer A.
strategy: For anti-TB drugs, memorize the signature toxicity of each: Ethambutol = Eyes (optic neuropathy), Rifampin = Red-orange fluids + CYP inducer, Isoniazid = hepatotoxicity + peripheral Neuropathy (give B6), Pyrazinamide = hepatotoxicity + hyperuricemia. When a side effect appears after starting RIPE, map the organ to the drug.

eliminations.label: isoniazid — peripheral, not optic, neuropathy
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: INH inhibits mycolic acid synthesis; its main toxicities are hepatotoxicity and PERIPHERAL neuropathy from B6 depletion. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it causes a stocking-glove sensory neuropathy, not optic neuropathy/central scotomas — and B6 (pyridoxine) is co-given to prevent it. ✅ Correct if: tingling/numbness in the hands and feet, or rising transaminases.

eliminations.label: pyrazinamide — liver and uric acid, not eyes
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: PZA's notable toxicities are hepatotoxicity and hyperuricemia (can flare gout). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it does not injure the optic nerve, so it can't explain bilateral scotomas. ✅ Correct if: an acute gout attack or elevated LFTs/uric acid during therapy.

eliminations.label: pyridoxine — protective vitamin, not toxic
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: pyridoxine is vitamin B6, added WITH isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: at this dose it is protective, not harmful, and does not cause optic neuropathy. ✅ Correct if: megadose B6 causing a sensory (not visual) neuropathy.

eliminations.label: rifampin — orange fluids, benign
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: rifampin inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase; it turns body fluids red-orange, can cause GI upset/cytopenias, and is a potent CYP450 inducer. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the orange discoloration is harmless and it does not cause optic neuropathy. ✅ Correct if: red-orange urine/tears or a drug-interaction (reduced OCP/warfarin effect).

reasoning: Chronic cough + night sweats + weight loss + travel to a TB-endemic area, RUL infiltrate, AFB+ = reactivation pulmonary TB on RIPE therapy
New bilateral central scotomas / blurred vision appear AFTER starting the drugs = a medication adverse effect
Among RIPE drugs, ethambutol causes dose-dependent optic neuropathy (decreased acuity, central scotoma, red-green color loss)
mechanism: Ethambutol inhibits arabinosyl transferase, blocking arabinogalactan synthesis in the mycobacterial cell wall. Its signature, dose- and duration-dependent toxicity is a retrobulbar/optic neuropathy that presents as decreased visual acuity, central scotomas, and red-green color blindness; it is usually reversible if caught early and the drug is stopped. This is why baseline and periodic visual acuity and color-vision testing are recommended during ethambutol therapy. The other RIPE drugs spare the optic nerve.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Pharm/Micro > anti-TB drugs (RIPE): ethambutol optic neuropathy, isoniazid (hepatotoxicity + peripheral neuropathy, give B6), rifampin (red-orange fluids, CYP inducer), pyrazinamide (hepatotoxicity, hyperuricemia). Memorize each drug's one signature toxicity.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Notice the pivot: the stem diagnoses TB, then tests DRUG TOXICITY. New bilateral central scotomas = optic neuropathy = the one TB drug that hits the eye (ethambutol).

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on central scotoma, optic neuropathy, acid-fast bacilli, reactivation TB, RIPE regimen, arabinosyl transferase? Tell me which term and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Isoniazid (B) tempts because it's the most famous TB drug with 'neuropathy' — but that's PERIPHERAL neuropathy (prevented by B6). The OPTIC neuropathy belongs to ethambutol.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank the pairing: EthamButol = Eyes. Map every RIPE side effect to its organ next time.

keywords.front: TB drug causing optic neuropathy / central scotoma / color blindness

firstAid.detail: Pharm > anti-TB: ethambutol inhibits arabinosyl transferase; dose-dependent optic neuropathy (decreased acuity, central scotoma, red-green color loss), usually reversible.
firstAid.location: Pharm > anti-TB: ethambutol inhibits arabinosyl transferase; dose-dependent optic neuropathy (decreased acuity, central scotoma, red-green color loss), usually reversible.

firstAid.detail: Pharm > isoniazid (mycolic acid; hepatotox + peripheral neuropathy, B6), rifampin (RNA pol; red-orange fluids, CYP inducer), pyrazinamide (hepatotox, hyperuricemia), pyridoxine (B6, protective).
firstAid.location: Pharm > isoniazid (mycolic acid; hepatotox + peripheral neuropathy, B6), rifampin (RNA pol; red-orange fluids, CYP inducer), pyrazinamide (hepatotox, hyperuricemia), pyridoxine (B6, protective).

firstAid.detail: Micro > M. tuberculosis: reactivation in upper lobes, AFB smear/culture, caseating granulomas; multidrug RIPE therapy and DOT.
firstAid.location: Micro > M. tuberculosis: reactivation in upper lobes, AFB smear/culture, caseating granulomas; multidrug RIPE therapy and DOT.

memoryHook: EthamButol = Eyes (optic neuritis, central scotoma). INH = peripheral Nerves (give B6).
